revision.text:
[[ar:عشرية (فلك مصري)]][[de:Dekade (Altägypten)]][[el:Δεκανοί]][[en:Decan]][[es:Decano (astronomía)]][[fr:Décan en Égypte antique]][[ko:십분각 (점성술)]][[pt:Decano (astronomia egípcia)]][[zh:旬星]] {{short description| part_ofs of stars in Ancient Egyptian astronomy|qq=}} {{about|decans in ancient astronomy|their use in astrology|Decan (astrology)|other uses|qq=}} [[File:Sarg des Nacht 3.jpg|thumb|upright=1.35|'Diagonal star table' from the late [[:en:11th Dynasty|11th Dynasty]] coffin lid; found at {{ill|Asyut|en|Asyut|qid=Q29962|lt=Asyut|1=Asyut}}, Egypt. {{ill|Roemer- und Pelizaeus-Museum Hildesheim|en|Roemer- und Pelizaeus-Museum Hildesheim|qid=Q896935|lt=Roemer- und Pelizaeus-Museum Hildesheim|1=Roemer- und Pelizaeus-Museum Hildesheim}}]] The '''decans''' ({{IPAc-en|ˈ|d|ɛ|k|ən|z|qq=}}; Egyptian ''bꜣktw'' or ''baktiu'', "[those] connected with work"{{sfn|Keyser|Scarborough|2018|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=TCxhDwAAQBAJ&pg=PT79 79]|qq=}}) are 36 groups of stars (small {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|qid=Q8928|lt=Constellation|1=Constellation}}s) used in the {{ill|Egyptian astronomy|en|Egyptian astronomy|qid=Q751843|lt=ancient Egyptian astronomy|1=Egyptian astronomy}} to conveniently divide the 360 degree ecliptic into 36 parts of 10 degrees each, both for [[:en:theurgical|theurgical]] and [[Heliacal rising|heliacal]] [[:en:horological|horological]] purposes. The decans each appeared, geocentrically, to rise consecutively on the horizon throughout each daily Earth rotation. The rising of each ''decan'' marked the beginning of a new decanal "{{ill|Hour|en|Hour|qid=Q25235|lt=Hour|1=Hour}}" (Greek ''hōra'') of the night for the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|qid=Q11768|lt=Ancient Egypt|1=Ancient Egypt}}ians, and they were used as a sidereal [[star clock]] beginning by at least the 9th or 10th Dynasty (c. 2100 BCE). Because a new decan also appears [[heliacal rising|heliacally]] every ten days (that is, every ten days, a new decanic star group reappears in the eastern sky at dawn right before the Sun rises, after a period of being obscured by the Sun's light), the ancient Greeks called them ''dekanoi'' (δεκανοί; pl. of δεκανός ''dekanos'') or "tenths". Decans gave way to a lunar division of 27 or 28 {{ill|lunar station|en|lunar station|qid=Q6413359|lt=lunar station|1=Lunar station}}s, also known as ''manzil'', lunar mansions or [[:en:nakshatras|nakshatras]] and thence to a {{ill|Zodiac|en|Zodiac|qid=Q40540|lt=Zodiac|1=Zodiac}} of 12 signs, based on an anthropomorphic pattern of constellations,{{sfn|Ptolemy|1940|qq=}} and their use can be seen in the {{ill|Dendera zodiac|en|Dendera zodiac|qid=Q3100139|lt=Dendera zodiac|1=Dendera zodiac}} dated to circa 50 BCE.{{sfn|Priskin|2016|qq=}} == Ancient Egyptian origins == [[File:Astronomical Ceiling, Tomb of Senenmut MET DT207429.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Astronomical ceiling of Senemut Tomb|Astronomical ceiling of Senemut Tomb]] showing various decans, as well as the personified representations of stars and constellations]] Decans first appeared in the [[:en:10th Dynasty|10th Dynasty]] (2100 BCE) on coffin lids.{{sfn|Symons|2013|qq=}} The sequence of these star patterns began with Sothis ({{ill|Sirius|en|Sirius|qid=Q3409|lt=Sirius|1=Sirius}}), and each decan contained a set of stars and corresponding divinities. As measures of time, the rising and setting of decans marked 'hours' and groups of 10 days which comprised an Egyptian year. The ancient ''{{ill|Book of Nut|en|Book of Nut|qid=Q2005559|lt=Book of Nut|1=Book of Nut}}'' covers the subject of the decans. There were 36 decans (36 × 10 = 360 days),{{sfn|von Bomhard|1999|p=51|qq=}} plus five added days to compose the 365 days of a solar based year. Decans measure {{ill|Sidereal time|en|Sidereal time|qid=Q191747|lt=Sidereal time|1=Sidereal time}} and the solar year is six hours longer; the Sothic and solar years in the Egyptian calendar realign every 1460 years. Decans represented on coffins from later dynasties (such as King {{ill|Seti I|en|Seti I|qid=Q157918|lt=Seti I|1=Seti I}}) compared with earlier decan images demonstrate the [[Sothic cycle|Sothic-solar shift]]. According to Sarah Symons:
Although we know the names of the decans, and in some cases can translate the names ('' '' means 'in the centre of the boat') the locations of the decanal stars and their relationships to modern star names and constellations are not known. This is due to many factors, but key problems are the uncertainty surrounding the observation methods used to develop and populate the diagonal star tables, and the criteria used to select decans (brightness, position, relationship with other stars, and so on).{{sfn|Symons|2007|qq=}}
==Later developments== These predictable heliacal re-appearances by the decans were eventually used by the Egyptians to mark the divisions of their annual solar {{ill|Calendar|en|Calendar|qid=Q12132|lt=Calendar|1=Calendar}}. Thus the heliacal rising of {{ill|Sirius|en|Sirius|qid=Q3409|lt=Sirius|1=Sirius}} marked the annual flooding of the Nile. This method led to a system of 12 daytime hours and 12 nighttime hours, varying in length according to the season. Later,{{when|date=September 2021|qq=}} a system of 24 "equinoctial" hours was used.{{sfnm|1a1=Neugebauer|1y=1983|1pp=205–209|2a1=Neugebauer|2y=1969|2pp=81–88|qq=}} After {{ill|Hellenistic astrology|en|Hellenistic astrology|qid=Q2068914|lt=Hellenistic astrology|1=Hellenistic astrology}} arose in Alexandria, recorded principally in the work of Claudius {{ill|Ptolemy|en|Ptolemy|qid=Q34943|lt=Ptolemy|1=Ptolemy}} and {{ill|Vettius Valens|en|Vettius Valens|qid=Q1051470|lt=Vettius Valens|1=Vettius Valens}}, various systems attributing symbolic significance to decans arose and linked these to the "[[:en:wandering stars|wandering stars]]" (Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter and Saturn) and the "Lights": (Sun and Moon).{{sfn|van der Waerden|1949|qq=}} Decans were connected, for example, with the winds, the four directions, the sect (day or night,) male and female, as well as the four humours (elements;) also these were hermetically considered linked with various diseases and with the timing for the engraving of talismans for curing them;{{sfn|Ruelle|1908|p=247|qq=}} with decanic "faces" (or "phases"), a system where three decans are assigned to each zodiacal sign, each covering 10° of the zodiac, and each ruled by a planetary ruler (see {{ill|Decan (astrology)|en|Decan (astrology)|qid=Q639904|lt=Decan (astrology)|1=Decan (astrology)}}); and correlated with astrological signs.{{sfn|Julius Firmicus Maternus|1968|loc=IV/22|qq=}} ==Descriptions of the decans== Decans are named in various Greco-Egyptian sources, many {{ill|Hermeticism|en|Hermeticism|qid=Q192933|lt=Hermetic|1=Hermeticism}} writings, the {{ill|Testament of Solomon|en|Testament of Solomon|qid=Q684055|lt=Testament of Solomon|1=Testament of Solomon}},{{harvnb|Gundel|1936|pp=77-81|qq=}}. the Tabula Aristobuli ({{ill|Codex Palatinus|en|Codex Palatinus|qid=Q5140228|lt=Codex Palatinus|1=Codex Palatinus}}) attributed to {{ill|Aristobulus of Paneas|en|Aristobulus of Paneas|qid=Q24068760|lt=Aristobulus of Paneas|1=Aristobulus of Paneas}},{{harvnb|Gundel|1936|pp=406-408|qq=}}. and the writings of {{ill|Julius Firmicus Maternus|en|Julius Firmicus Maternus|qid=Q367422|lt=Julius Firmicus Maternus|1=Julius Firmicus Maternus}}, {{ill|Cosmas of Maiuma|en|Cosmas of Maiuma|qid=Q2496948|lt=Cosmas of Maiuma|1=Cosmas of Maiuma}}, {{ill|Joseph Justus Scaliger|en|Joseph Justus Scaliger|qid=Q315163|lt=Joseph Justus Scaliger|1=Joseph Justus Scaliger}}, and {{ill|Athanasius Kircher|en|Athanasius Kircher|qid=Q76738|lt=Athanasius Kircher|1=Athanasius Kircher}}. Images of the decans are described in Hermetic writings, by the Indian astrologer {{ill|Varāhamihira|en|Varāhamihira|qid=Q858348|lt=Varāhamihira|1=Varāhamihira}}, in the {{ill|Picatrix|en|Picatrix|qid=Q596932|lt=Picatrix|1=Picatrix}}, and in Japanese writings.{{sfn|Gundel|1936|pp=223-225|qq=}} Varāhamihira's images of the decans was influenced by Greco-Egyptian, if not Hermetic, depictions of the decans by way of the {{ill|Yavanajataka|en|Yavanajataka|qid=Q8050366|lt=Yavanajataka|1=Yavanajataka}}.{{sfn|Pingree|1963|qq=}} Their role in Japanese astrology may have derived from an earlier Chinese{{harvnb|Gundel|1936|pp=217-221|qq=}}. or Indian form{{sfn|Michio|2008|qq=}} possibly from adding the twelve animals of the {{ill|Chinese zodiac|en|Chinese zodiac|qid=Q1976204|lt=Chinese zodiac|1=Chinese zodiac}} to a list of twenty-four hour stars. They were most common between the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura period}} and {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}s.{{sfn|Michio|2008|qq=}} The first original decan position due to the precession in ancient times started at 0° of Cancer when the [[Heliacal rising|heliacal rising of Sirius]] (Egyptian Sepdet; Greco-Egyptian: Sothis) before sunrise marking the Egyptian New Year which fell at 0° of Leo at July 20 in the Julian calendar, that is July 22/23 on the Gregorian calendar.{{Citation needed|date=November 2016|qq=}} {| class="wikitable" |+Names of the Decans |- ! Western Zodiac ! Decan ! Original Decan Position due to the Precession in Ancient Times ! Ancient Egyptian
({{ill|E. A. Wallis Budge|en|E. A. Wallis Budge|qid=Q2316289|lt=Budge|1=E. A. Wallis Budge}}){{sfn|Budge|1904|pp=304-308|qq=}} ! Greco-Egyptian{{sfn|Budge|1904|pp=304-308|qq=}} ! Testament of Solomon{{sfn|Gundel|1936|pp=49-62|qq=}}{{sfn|Conybeare|1898|qq=}} ! Aristobulus's names ! Greek Hermeticism{{sfn|Ruelle|1908|qq=}}{{harvnb|Gundel|1936|pp=374-383|qq=}}. ! Latin Hermeticism{{sfn|Hermes Trismegistus|1993|pp=iii-12|qq=}} ! Firmicus ! Cosmas{{sfn|Gundel|1936|pp=353-354|qq=}} ! Scalinger ! Χont-har | Rhyax or Ruax | Bendonc | Chenlachori | Aulathamas | Senator or Asiccan | {{ill|Aidoneus|en|Aidoneus|qid=Q3605510|lt=Aidoneus|1=Aidoneus}} | Asiccan | [[Horus#Heru-ur (Horus the Elder)|Arueris]] (ⲁⲣⲟⲩⲉⲣⲓⲥ) |- | 2 | 29 | P11-d:nw-sbA Qeṭ | W17-Xr:r-sbA ⲭⲟⲛⲧⲁⲭⲣⲉ

Si-sesme | Akton | Alleinac | Aphebis | Psermes | Sentineu or Aterceni(-cem) | Kairos (repeated) | Senicer | Panotragus (ⲡⲁⲛⲟⲧⲣⲁⲅⲟⲥ) |- |rowspan="3"| {{ill|Sagittarius (astrology)|en|Sagittarius (astrology)|qid=Q2194186|lt=Sagittarius|1=Sagittarius (astrology)}} | 25 |16 | Hr:Z1-ib-P1-sbA Ḥer-ab-uȧa | Hr:Z1-ib-P1-sbA ʿⲣⲏⲟⲩⲱ
Hre-ua | Anatreth | Ortusa | Sebos | Clinothois | Eregbuo or Ergbuo | Loimos (repeated) | Eregbuo | Tolmophta (ⲧⲟⲗⲙⲟⲫϯ) |- | 26 |17 | P6-W8:??:W10-V17-Ba16-S-s-m-w-sbA-Ba16a Shesmu | z:S-mw-sbA ⲥⲉⲥⲙⲉ, ⲥⲓⲥⲉⲥⲙⲉ
Sesme | Enautha or Enenuth | Daha | Teuchmos | Thursois | Sagon | {{ill|Persephone|en|Persephone|qid=Q45967|lt=Kore|1=Persephone}} | Sagen | Tomras (ⲧⲟⲙⲣⲁⲥ) |- | 27 |18 | k:n-m-w-sbA Kenmu | k:n-Aa15:sbA ⲕⲟⲛⲓⲙⲉ
Konime | Axesbyth or Phêth | Satan | Chthisar | Renethis | Chenene or Chenem | [[:en:Ananke (mythology)|Ananke]] | Chenen | Teraph (ⲧⲉⲣⲁⲫ) |- |rowspan="3"| {{ill|Capricorn (astrology)|en|Capricorn (astrology)|qid=Q164272|lt=Capricorn|1=Capricorn (astrology)}} | 28 |19 | s-m-d:t*sbA Semṭet | s-Aa15:t*sbA ⲥⲙⲁⲧ
Smat | Hapax or Harpax | Eracto | Tair | Renpsois | Themeso | [[:en:Asklepios|Asklepios]] | Themeso | Soda (ⲥⲱⲇⲁ) |- | 29 |20 | tp:a-s-m-d:sbA Ṭepā-semṭ | tp:a-s-Aa15:t*sbA . . . . . . . .
Srat | Anoster | Salac | Epitek | Manethois | Epiemu or Epimen | {{ill|Hygieia|en|Hygieia|qid=Q202257|lt=Hygieia|1=Hygieia}} | Epima | Riruphta (ⲣⲓⲣⲟⲩⲫϯ) |- | 30 |21 | z:r:t-E11-sbA Sert | z:r-t:sbA ⲥⲣⲱ
Si-srat | Physikoreth or Alleborith | Seros | Epichnaus | Marcois | Omot | Tolma | Homoth | Monuphta (ⲙⲟⲛⲟⲩⲫϯ) |- |rowspan="3"| {{ill|Aquarius (astrology)|en|Aquarius (astrology)|qid=Q162119|lt=Aquarius|1=Aquarius (astrology)}} | 31 |22 | zA-zA-z:r:t-sbA Sasa-sert | zA-z:r:t*sbA ⲥⲓⲥⲣⲱ
Tpa-χu | Aleureth or Hephesimireth | Tonghel | Isi | Ularis | Oro or Asoer | {{ill|Dike (mythology)|en|Dike (mythology)|qid=Q465875|lt=Dike|1=Dike (mythology)}} | Oroasoer | Brondeus (ⲃⲣⲟⲛⲇⲉⲟⲩⲥ) |- | 32 |23 | Xr:r-K23-z:r:t*sbA Kher-khept-sert | Xr:r-x*p:t-z:r:sbA . . . . . . . .
Xu | Ichthion | Anafa | Sosomo | Luxois | Cratero or Astiro | {{ill|Phobos (mythology)|en|Phobos (mythology)|qid=Q105019|lt=Phobos|1=Phobos (mythology)}} | Astiro | Vucula (ⲃⲟⲩⲕⲟⲩⲗⲁ) |- | 33 |24 | Ax-Ax-sbA Khukhu | tp:a-N8-sbA ⲧⲡⲏⲭⲩ
Tpa-Biu | Achoneoth or Agchoniôn | Simos | Chonoumous | Crauxes | Tepis or Amasiero | {{ill|Osiris|en|Osiris|qid=Q46491|lt=Osiris|1=Osiris}} | Tepisatras | {{ill|Proteus|en|Proteus|qid=Q191481|lt=Proteus|1=Proteus}} (ⲡⲣⲟⲧⲉⲟⲩⲥ) |- |rowspan="3"| {{ill|Pisces (astrology)|en|Pisces (astrology)|qid=Q1254190|lt=Pisces|1=Pisces (astrology)}} | 34 |25 | bA-bA Baba | N8-w-ra-sbA ⲭⲩ
Biu | Autoth or Autothith | Achaf | Tetimo | Fambais | Acha or Atapiac | [[:en:Okeanos|Okeanos]] | Archatapias | {{ill|Remphan|en|Remphan|qid=Q3649968|lt=Rephan|1=Remphan}} (ⲣⲉⲫⲁⲛ) |- | 35 |26 | W17-Hr:r-w-sbA Khent-ḥeru | tp:a-G30-sbA ⲧⲡⲏⲃⲓⲟⲩ
Xont-Har | Phtheneoth or Phthenoth | Larvata | Sopphi | Flugmois | Tepibui or Tepabiu | [[:en:Dolus (mythology)|Dolus]] | Thopibui | Sourut (ⲥⲟⲟⲩⲣⲟⲩⲧ) |- | 36 |27 | Hr:Z1-ib-W17-n:t*w-sbA Ḥer-ȧb-khentu | [[-G30-Z3-sbA-]] ⲃⲓⲟⲩ, ⲧⲡⲓⲃⲓⲟⲩ
Tpi-biu | Bianakith | Ajaras | Syro | Piatris | Uiu or Aatexbui | {{ill|Elpis (mythology)|en|Elpis (mythology)|qid=Q2633131|lt=Elpis|1=Elpis (mythology)}} | Atembui | Phallophorus (ⲫⲁⲗⲗⲟⲫⲟⲣⲟⲥ) |- |} == Ancient India == {{main|Drekkana|qq=}} In India, the division of the zodiac into 36 ten degree portions is called either the {{ill|drekkana|en|drekkana|qid=Q17002353|lt=drekkana|1=Drekkana}} (drekkāṇa), the dreshkana (dreṣkāṇa), or the drikana (dṛkāṇa).Monier Williams Sanskrit Dictionary{{full citation needed|date=January 2024|qq=}} The iconography and use of the drekkanas is mention earliest by [[:en:Sphujidhvaja|Sphujidhvaja]] in {{ill|Yavanajataka|en|Yavanajataka|qid=Q8050366|lt=Yavanajataka|1=Yavanajataka}} (269–270 CE), and given detailed treatment by [[:en:Varahamihira|Varahamihira]] in his [[:en:Brihat-Samhita|Brihat-Samhita]] (550 CE). Modern scholars believe the decans were imported into India through the Greeks, who learned about them from the Egyptians.{{sfn|Pingree|1963|qq=}} ==See also== *{{ill|Asterism (astronomy)|en|Asterism (astronomy)|qid=Q9262|lt=Asterism (astronomy)|1=Asterism (astronomy)}} *[[:en:Astronomical ceiling of Senemut Tomb|Astronomical ceiling of Senemut Tomb]] *{{ill|Behenian fixed star|en|Behenian fixed star|qid=Q4645995|lt=Behenian fixed star|1=Behenian fixed star}} *{{ill|Chinese zodiac|en|Chinese zodiac|qid=Q1976204|lt=Chinese zodiac|1=Chinese zodiac}} *{{ill|Palazzo Schifanoia|en|Palazzo Schifanoia|qid=Q1276962|lt=Palazzo Schifanoia|1=Palazzo Schifanoia}}, Ferrara *{{ill|Worship of heavenly bodies|en|Worship of heavenly bodies|qid=Q3627640|lt=Worship of heavenly bodies|1=Worship of heavenly bodies}} ==References== {{Reflist|qq=}} ===Works cited=== {{refbegin|2|indent=yes|qq=}} *{{cite book |url=https://archive.org/details/godsofegyptianso02budg |title=The gods of the Egyptians; or, Studies in Egyptian mythology |last=Budge |first=E. A. Wallis |author-link=E. A. Wallis Budge |date=1904 |place=Chicago |publisher=Open Court Publishing Co.|qq=}} *{{cite journal |last=Conybeare |first=Frederick Cornwallis (tr.) |author-link=Frederick Cornwallis Conybeare|date=October 1898 |title=The Testament of Solomon |journal=The Jewish Quarterly Review |volume=XI |issue=1 |pages=1–45 |doi=10.2307/1450398 |jstor=1450398|qq=}} *{{cite book |title=Dekane und Dekansterbilder |first=Wilhelm |last=Gundel |publisher=J. L. |author-link1=Bartel Leendert van der Waerden |title=Babylonian Astronomy. II. The Thirty-Six Stars |journal=Journal of Near Eastern Studies |volume=8 |issue=1 |pages=6–26 |date=January 1949 |jstor=542436 |quote=The property of the Chaldean Decans that one of them rose every ten days made them fit to be assimilated to the Egyptian decans. This assimilation was performed in the decan lists of Hellenistic astrology. |doi=10.1086/370901 |s2cid=222443741|qq=}} * {{cite book |last=von Bomhard |first=Anna-Sophie |title=The Egyptian Calendar: A Work for Eternity |place=London |publisher=Periplus |year=1999|qq=}} {{refend|qq=}} ==Further reading== {{refbegin|2|indent=yes|qq=}} *{{cite book |first=Paul |last=Christian |year=1969 |orig-year=1870 |chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/historypracticeo01chri/page/476/mode/2up |chapter=The Thirty-Six Decans |title=The History and Practice of Magic |publisher=Citadel |pages=476–478 |qq=}} *{{cite book|last1=Symons|first1=Sarah|editor1-last=Ruggles|editor1-first=Clive L.N.|title=Handbook of Archaeoastronomy and Ethnoastronomy|date=2014|publisher=Springer|location=New York|isbn=978-1-4614-6140-1|pages=1495–1500|chapter=Egyptian 'Star Clocks'|qq=}} {{refend|qq=}} == External links == *[http://aea.physics.mcmaster.ca/ Ancient Egyptian Astronomy Database] (Symons, Cockcroft, Bettencourt & Koykka, 2013) *[http://ib205.tripod.com/decans.html Egypt Astronomy/Astrology: The decans] [[wikt:ḥrj jb#Adjective|ḥry-ỉb]] [[wikt:wjꜣ|wỉꜣ]] [[en:Decan]] [[ar:عشرية (فلك مصري)]] [[de:Dekade (Altägypten)]] [[el:Δεκανοί]] [[es:Decano (astronomía)]] [[fr:Décan en Égypte antique]] [[ko:십분각 (점성술)]] [[pt:Decano (astronomia egípcia)]] [[zh:旬星]] {{wikidata link|Q5248738}} [[Category:Ancient astronomy]] [[Category:Asterisms (astronomy)]] [[Category:Constellations]] [[Category:Egyptian calendar]] [[Category:Technical factors of Chinese astrology]] [[Category:Technical factors of Western astrology]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
